Kelly Crosbie’s Post

Kelly Crosbie
1y
  • Report this post

NCDHHS Invests $20 Million in Non-Law Enforcement Transportation for Mental Health Crises NCDHHS has released a RFP to establish non-law enforcement transportation services for those with mental health and substance use crisis needs.  This initiative provides trauma-informed transportation from emergency rooms to treatment facilities, offering a safe and therapeutic alternative to traditional law enforcement transport. By decriminalizing and destigmatizing the process of seeking mental health care, we aim to encourage more individuals to access the support they need without fear. The program will initially operate in two regions, selected through a competitive request for proposal (RFP) process. Eligible transportation vendors can apply for the RFP via the NCDHHS website here: https://lnkd.in/eeibQ-4G  This effort reflects our commitment to transforming mental health crisis response in North Carolina, ensuring compassionate care for all. Read more in the press release: https://lnkd.in/eCNGVQSb

    Understanding the complexities of the Baker Act is crucial for safeguarding individual rights during a mental health crisis. This statute empowers the state of Florida to take temporary custody of individuals deemed in need of immediate services due to mental health concerns. Once detained, the person's ability to make medical or mental health decisions is suspended, initiating an involuntary hold that lasts up to 72 hours after medical clearance. However, weekends and holidays can extend this period, turning a three-day hold into potentially six days without due process. Swift action is vital to restore rights and enable individuals to choose their preferred services. Without intervention, a facility can file a petition for placement, extending detention up to 11 days. During this time, the individual remains without rights and due process. Understanding the legal intricacies of the Baker Act can prevent prolonged detention and ensure that loved ones receive appropriate care and support. #bakeract #mentalhealthcrisis #floridalaw #involuntarydetention #patientrights #dueprocess #mentalhealthawareness #legalsystem #custodylaw #healthservices https://lnkd.in/gseuQqzK https://lnkd.in/ePArHVCP

      Our Associate Director, Gabriela Zapata-Alma, LCSW CADC, recently provided expert commentary to News 4 Investigates (WIVB Buffalo) on the systemic challenges survivors face when seeking help from law enforcement and emergency response systems. In response to a tragic case that underscores the life-or-death stakes of timely, trauma-informed intervention, Gabriela highlighted a troubling pattern: "Unfortunately, very often, when people reach out for help, the help just isn't there, it doesn't actually end up helping, and a lot of times when survivors reach out for help, they actually encounter more danger." They emphasized the fundamental need for systems transformation: "We need to do our best to respond and, above all else, we need to believe survivors. Far too often, our systems that are meant to protect survivors end up re-traumatizing them, re-victimizing them." When survivors reach out for help, the response they receive—or fail to receive—can determine their safety and survival. This case illustrates why NCDVTMH's work at the intersections of domestic violence, trauma, mental health, and substance use remains essential. Key Takeaways for Systems and Communities: ▫️Prioritize rapid response to domestic violence calls ▫️Believe survivors who reach out for help ▫️Implement trauma-informed practices across all response systems ▫️Recognize that inadequate responses increase danger for survivors Read the full article: https://ow.ly/Svvg50X6VgW Learn more about our work transforming systems to better support survivors: https://ow.ly/H47f50X6VgY

      Happy World Mental Health Day! Today is a major step forward for behavioral health crisis response! U.S. Representatives Ansari and Watson-Coleman have introduced the Mental Health Emergency Responder Act — a groundbreaking federal bill to help communities build non-law-enforcement behavioral health crisis response programs across the country. The Act creates a competitive grant program through the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services to fund local governments, Tribal nations, certified community behavioral health clinics, and nonprofit partners to: ✅ Recruit, train, and equip behavioral health responders and paramedics. ✅ Integrate co-response teams into 911 and 988 systems. ✅ Expand clinician- and paramedic-led mobile crisis teams. ✅ Educate communities about alternatives to police-led crisis response. This legislation recognizes that a mental health emergency is a health emergency — NOT A CRIMINAL ONE! Every day, far too many families experience tragedy when the wrong system responds first. By supporting local innovation and compassionate care, this bill helps communities create crisis systems that save lives and restore trust. The Mental Health Emergency Responder Act represents a new kind of federal leadership — one that invests in public health, public safety, and dignity. Let’s make sure every child in crisis gets the right response at the right time, from the right team. #MentalHealth #PublicHealth #CrisisResponse #988Lifeline #BehavioralHealth #PolicyChange #HealthEquity #CommunityCare

      💡 What is co-response, and what does a successful co-responder program look like? ➡️ A co-response model pairs law enforcement agencies with human services organizations to provide tailored response to certain calls for service. ➡️ The goal of co-responder programs is to divert arrests and de-escalate crises involving mental and behavioral health, substance use, and quality-of-life issues. ➡️ Jurisdictions deploying co-responder teams report reduced use of force, arrests, hospitalizations, and officer time on scene.     • Mark G. Astor, Esq.
      8mo
      • Report this post

      Navigating the complexities of mental health crises can be daunting, and that's where the Baker Act steps in as a crucial statute in Florida. Designed to provide temporary custody for those in urgent need of emergency services, the Baker Act can be a life-saving intervention when executed correctly. The accompanying video illustrates the impact of proper implementation by law enforcement, demonstrating its potential to be a transformative tool in mental health emergencies. However, the process isn’t foolproof. When mishandled, individuals can unfortunately fall through the cracks, becoming invisible within the system. This underscores the importance of vigilance and advocacy for loved ones who might find themselves subject to the Baker Act. Whether enacted legally or otherwise, it’s vital to act swiftly to ensure they receive the appropriate care and support. Collective awareness and understanding can make all the difference in turning a crisis into a pathway for recovery and healing. #bakeract #mentalhealthawareness #floridalaw #emergencyservices #lifesavingtool #lawenforcement #mentalhealthcrisis #supportsystem #advocacy #mentalhealthmatters https://lnkd.in/e5n3QWP7 https://lnkd.in/gseuQqzK https://lnkd.in/ePArHVCP

        Across the country, cities are strengthening their health and safety networks by deploying trained, unarmed professionals to respond to low-risk 911 calls related to mental health, substance use and other needs. Since 2020, community responder programs have expanded from six to nearly 90, now handling more than 200,000 calls each year. On World Mental Health Day, we are proud to highlight how this growing network of care is helping people get the support they need—and making cities safer and more connected. Our grantee, Law Enforcement Action Partnership (LEAP), tracks the progress of these programs through a national map and resource hub. Explore the map and learn more: https://bit.ly/4nSE6xC #WorldMentalHealthDay #MentalHealth #AlternativeResponse #CriminalJustice
